summary: Jeff Atwood argues that voice recognition technology has stagnated at around 80% accuracy since 2001, far below the 96-98% human transcription baseline, making it impractical for general use. He challenges the assumption that voice control would even be desirable, noting that spoken communication is inherently inefficient, places burden on the listener, and tends to be rambling and unclear. Through examples like YouTube's automatic captions and podcast transcription attempts, he demonstrates that machine-generated transcripts create more work to correct than starting fresh. He draws a parallel to handwriting recognition, which was once hyped but quietly disappeared from mainstream devices, suggesting voice recognition may follow the same trajectory.
key_points: Speech recognition accuracy has been stuck at 80% since 2001 despite massive increases in computing power, while human transcription achieves 96-98%
Voice communication is inherently inefficient: it's verbose, listeners only absorb half of what's said, and it burdens the receiver more than text
Correcting a machine transcript full of errors is often more frustrating than transcribing from scratch
A room full of people voice-controlling their computers would be both impractical and cacophonous
Handwriting recognition followed a hype-to-obscurity arc that voice recognition may also follow
money_quotes: With human discrimination as high as 98%, the unclosed gap left little basis for conversation.
Is voice control, even hypothetically perfect voice control, more effective than the lower tech alternatives?
Voice communication remains filled with pitfalls and misunderstandings. Even in the best of conditions.
What's worse: transcribing everything by hand, from scratch – or correcting every third or fourth word in an auto-generated machine transcript?
I'm also trying to imagine a room full of people controlling their computers or phones using their voices. Wouldn't we all end up hoarse and deaf?

More than ten years later, you'd expect to see massively improved handwriting recognition of some sort in today's iPads and iPhones, right? Well, maybe, if by 'massively improved' you mean 'nonexistent.'
key_insight: The fundamental problem with voice control isn't the technology's accuracy—it's that spoken communication is an inherently inefficient medium that we shouldn't want to extend to our computers.
